attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman is admitted to hospital with fever and dysuria. She has been an inpatient for the past three weeks following complicated abdominal surgery and has had an indwelling urinary catheter in place since admission. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease. Her medications include insulin and metformin. She has received multiple courses of broad-spectrum antibiotics during her admission.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. Her abdomen is soft and non-tender. The urinary catheter is in situ with cloudy urine in the bag. Urine microscopy shows >100 white blood cells per high power field. Blood and urine cultures grow Enterococcus faecium resistant to vancomycin.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with catheter-associated urosepsis caused by vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium (VRE). The patient has several risk factors for VRE infection, including prolonged hospitalisation, urinary catheterisation, recent surgery, and previous broad-spectrum antibiotic use.

Linezolid is an appropriate choice for treating VRE infections. It has good activity against VRE and achieves adequate urinary concentrations. It can be administered orally or intravenously, and therapeutic drug monitoring is not required. However, it can cause myelosuppression, particularly with prolonged use.

Vancomycin plus gentamicin would be ineffective as the organism is resistant to vancomycin. While gentamicin can sometimes be used synergistically with other agents for enterococcal infections, it is not appropriate as monotherapy.

Meropenem, despite being a broad-spectrum antibiotic, does not have reliable activity against enterococci, including VRE. Carbapenems are not appropriate for treating enterococcal infections.

Piperacillin-tazobactam would not be effective against VRE. While it has activity against some enterococci, VRE strains are typically resistant to beta-lactam antibiotics.

Ceftriaxone and other cephalosporins have poor activity against enterococci in general and would not be effective against VRE.

Alternative treatment options for VRE infections include daptomycin, which would be particularly useful in bacteraemia, though it is ineffective for respiratory tract infections as it is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant. The choice between linezolid and daptomycin often depends on the site of infection, local resistance patterns, and patient factors such as renal function. In addition to antibiotic therapy, the urinary catheter should be removed or replaced as part of the management plan.
learningPoints: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE) requires contact precautions to prevent transmission in healthcare settings.
Linezolid and daptomycin are appropriate treatment options for VRE infections, though daptomycin is ineffective for respiratory infections.
Risk factors for VRE colonisation include prolonged hospitalisation, previous antibiotic use, and immunosuppression.
Catheter-associated urinary tract infections are a common source of healthcare-associated infections and should prompt catheter removal if possible.
references: Antibiotic. In: Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; accessed {26 Oct 2024}. https://www.tg.org.au

Cairns KA, Udy AA, Peel TN, Abbott IJ, Dooley MJ, Peleg AY. Therapeutics for Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcal Bloodstream Infections. Clin Microbiol Rev. 2023;36(2):e0005922. doi:10.1128/cmr.00059-22
